item.title: Christians Should Not Use Social Media
item.description:
Social Media - Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, Tik Tok, Snapchat, and whatever the hottest new social media platform of the day is - they're quite popular. Many people use them, and they're ostensibly useful for their purpose - allowing users to share text and media with a select group of other users, with relative levels of broadcast. There are several general purposes to services like this:

"show me what I want to see" (subscriptions, follows, friends)
"share how I want to share" (make public posts visible to all, keep private messages private)
"allow me to discover new things" (search for people, topics, and other data)

However, that's just surface-level. Everyone also knows that these services are built to be addicting on purpose. They collect every bit of data they can, even data users don't think they provide, and tailor services based on this data for maximum retention and engagement; simply put they learn from users and display content that keeps users on the site as long as possible. Not what the user actually wants to see, but what keeps him or her scrolling. This is a clear violation of the purpose of the service - "show me what I ask to see".

The service also retains copies of deleted data, has the ability to browse "private" messages and posts, and often hides the posts of a user to their fellow users if those posts won't keep users on the service. Censorship and message alteration is a normal feature of these types of services. A user's content may be flagged, leading to "shadowbans" or outright removal from the service. If a post intended for a small audience would be useful to the service for retention, it will be heavily promoted and "go viral". This lack of clarity about how things are actually shared is an obvious violation of another principle, "share how I want to share".

Social media services never fail to promote discovery, but it's usually only posts and accounts that are deemed "safe", not really in line with what the user wants to see. Worse, there's no way to tailor this discovery or put on reasonable restrictions, like "don't show me softcore pornography" or "no politics". For most social media services, the things users will be able to search for and discover are either massively popular already, or pre-vetted, not-yet-banned topics that drive engagement. With this the third goal, "allow me to discover new things", is also void.

Most social media sites don't make good on their surface-level promises. They pretend that they do, but instead fulfill their own goals of exploiting users for profit. If it's not clear by now, social media platforms are tools for evil. If a platform is built to do evil, and the operator is using it for evil deeds, should Christians even consider using it at all? I've heard people argue that posting good Christian messages on these platforms is "using the platform against itself" - seems like a noble goal. But continuing to use it at all will continue to drive more people to sign up, if that's where the message is available. All of this usage only benefits the social media service in the end. More users to addict, more data to gather, more ads to sell, more minds and hearts to change. And what are the chances that the platform will only promote other Christian messages to Christian users? How long will those truly Christian messages be allowed to stay around? Casting Christian pearls before the swine of social media platforms is arguably equivalent to preaching the gospel to Sodomites. Not even God could find ten good men there.

We're well beyond needing to provide evidence of the harm social media has done to the world. You, reader, could probably name more than I could. The injustice of this type of worldly attraction, despite its attractiveness, must not be something Christians succumb to. We are discerning when it comes to books, music, schools, dress, relationships, our words, our food and drink, consumer goods, and how we treat this planet. So why the blindness against technology, and technological services?

I call my fellow Christians to give up social media. Not just Facebook and Twitter, but all forms of this pox upon society. The surface-level use case of these services - communication and sharing of data on the Internet - can be achieved in many different ways using technology that is simpler, and not designed for evil.

item.title: No Signal
item.description:
I have deleted my Signal account, after years of it being my primary messaging system, and years of fervent encouragement of others to use it.

Signal was a simple, secure, open source messaging program. It's turned into a service with contact discovery, a cryptocurrency wallet, and now, centralized social media. That's not a thing that I want to use.

This is all in an effort to increase the reach, get more users, have more people using Signal instead of Telegram or WhatsApp or whatever. Sure, I get it. But I don't want to use Telegram or WhatsApp or anything that looks like it. I don't want apps. I don't want social media. I don't want a smartphone. I want a computer program that offers encrypted communication via a standard, decentralized, ubiquitous protocol. That's it.

If you'd like to reach me, send an XMPP message to my email address, or call my phone number, or send me an email.

A side benefit of this: I've lost the final reason to use a smartphone.

I'm free!

item.title: Short Smartphone Rant
item.description:
Here's a fun thing for you to test: try playing 2 simultaneous audio streams on Android or iOS.

Having trouble? That (and other countless things) are surprisingly limited on mobile devices in comparison to a real PC.

The pinephone and many other devices (thanks to postmarketOS) can be full desktop computers. One can choose to run a full desktop environment like GNOME and XFCE, but most prefer to run Phosh, a mobile-optimized/stripped-down GNOME, or SXMO, a mobile-optimized gesture-based tiling window manager setup. The pinephone has an optional keyboard attachment - it's a bit chunky, but still pocketable. It's a real computer. I have full access! I can delete the kernel and wipe the modem if I want. I can uninstall any software I want and replace it with whatever I want. I can build apps for the device on the device. I can dock it to USB-C and get display output.

If we accept that smartphones are the norm, sealed-in batteries, no SD card slot, no headphone jack, no hardware killswitches, arbitrarily limited features, proprietary drivers, brutally short software lifecycles, and every other of the dozen+ ways to violate user freedom that smartphones have pioneered, we will continue to suffocate computing from the bottom up. Look at how hard it is to replace batteries on modern laptops. Didn't use to be that way. Same with RAM and SSDs - they're becoming built-in. This is a nightmare for upgradability and long-term repairability/usability. The best way to help the environment is to upgrade your stuff, not throw it away. Extend its life for years and years. That's why "reduce" and "reuse" come BEFORE "recycle".

This new wave of mini PCs with open hardware and software is setting things right. They are way better than smartphones, even if they're "worse". They're worse at being proprietary sealed-in trash-waiting-to-happen environment-destroyers that funnel you towards just consuming content on five different approved websites. They're probably a bit worse at being telephones and televisions, but those aren't what we want. They're better at being computers for computer hackers, which is what we want.

item.title: Simple Computing
item.description:
Two articles, one about analogies and the other on everlasting desktops, both got me thinking. This is why I follow blogs, and why I write one (despite the low readership) - it helps me think about things I care about.

Computer literacy among the general population is massively low. My mom used Windows XP to great success, and my dad installed a graphics card+drivers into that same computer after hooking up home broadband and setting up a Wi-Fi router. Now, neither knows the difference between a web browser and a search engine, or an email program and email service provider. It's app this and iPad that - their general computer literacy is completely gone.

It's not just my parents' gradual slide into ignorance - the new generations aren't literate either. I grew up around slow, obtuse, and failure-prone computers. They didn't do everything. They required lots of manual intervention. The Internet wasn't ubiquitous. My cohorts and I had to learn how to use them. There were no hints, no hand-holding. Remember the search function in Windows XP with the little dog? It took ages to run, locked up the whole machine, and didn't even work! Older systems I used didn't even have that.

I've identified two main problems causing this lack of literacy: shifting sands and complicated, black-box computers.

The constantly shifting landscape of modern software and hardware doesn't do anyone favors. Software changes so often that it's hard for users to learn how to use anything before its auto-updates have shape-shifted it into something unrecognizable. Users pick platforms, because that's what they're tricked into, and the rug is ripped out from under them before you can say "pick an open protocol instead of a centralized platform". This churn seems to have many people throwing in the towel, using things in anger that they'd rather not be touching, and feeling stuck with software and services that bubblewrap functionality and baby users. iPads are peak fisher-price computer. No filesystems, no shell access, no installing software, heck, only recently could one even pick a different default web browser! These things masquerade as real computers but abstract away (to the harm of the user) critical functionality for all but the most basic of users. Not only that, all the built-in software changes all the time, but since the user has no control, and you can't go back to the old software, users are forced to re-learn the same basic concepts year after year.

Newer computers might as well be black boxes. Although the lower-end desperate-fool-tier PCs you find at best buy still may have some visible screws, most PCs are getting less repairable than ever. CPUs have gone from socketed and upgradable to soldered on the board. Power sockets, which can get loose over time, used to be easily replaceable 5-dollar parts, but are now USB-C and soldered on the motherboard. Wi-Fi chips are no longer replaceable. And, forget complaining about soldered-on memory; that's so 2012. Apple computers even solder on the main storage now! Consumer-grade computers used to come with manuals, and now you'll be lucky if you can open the computer without dusting off your Security Torx or proprietary pentalobe driver. Of course enthusiasts still can and do build their own rigs, and many enterprise machines (like HP Z-series desktops, costing many thousands of dollars and usually sold per-pallet to engineering firms) have instructions printed on the inside panel or come with Thinkpad-tier assembly manual. But the average user's got a Personal Computer that can't be Personalized. If you want different than what came out of the box on day one - too bad!

Both of these factors seem to have led users to distrust and dislike computers, treating them like simple tools instead of a personal treasure trove of infinite possibility. After all, if every machine you've owned for the last ten years doesn't even really belong to you, then at some point you stop caring about it, and just carry on. It's sad to see so many people despise smartphones and iPads and modern Windows PCs and Macs yet continuing to carry them around.

The better way is a simpler computer. When users have less changing, less to manage, and less surprises, it's easier to understand and get comfortable with the computer. A simple Linux or BSD system with a classic graphical environment like Xfce and some basic programs will actually empower the users instead of babying them. Simple programs that don't change much, with easy configuration and good documentation, will make all the difference. Only when the computer is a helpful tool does it actually become interesting again.

The advertising garbage in Windows and Android and bubblewrapped baby-world of Apple are what frustrate users, not "computers" in general. The constant change and blackbox nature of these modern devices don't make it easy - they make it hard. Abstraction layering doesn't make a hard task easy. It makes a hard task even harder. Actual simplicity is what makes things simple and easy. I'm doing my part and helping people switch to simple, easy, and understandable. It really is making lives better.

item.title: Star Wars Article
item.description: My wife and I co-authored an article on how to make Star Wars better. It's almost as much fun as the time we calculated the economic impact of Gaston's egg consumption. I might upload that at some point, but the Star Wars thing is here.

item.title: It Keeps Happening
item.description:
The technocracy only gets worse and worse!

This is a story about some dads who were concerned for their young sons' welfare. In two separate and unrelated cases, a dad took pictures of his naked son to send to doctors for a diagnosis; in 2020/1, in-person doctor visits had to be for emergencies. Shortly after, the dad's email, photo backup, chat, and phone stopped working. Thanks Google! The police even went after the poor concerned fathers. (Of course they were both innocent.)

A Google spokeswoman said the company stands by its decisions, even though law enforcement cleared the two men. This is an autocratic tone-deaf no-forgiveness policy written by people who have no children. Apple attempted to do the same exact thing last year, but there was a much bigger outcry against it, so they didn't do it. (Or maybe they did. We will never know. iCloud is non-free and the code is private to Apple.)

Stop using Apple and Google, right now. These companies will stop at nothing to enforce policies that harm innocent people, invade privacy, censor and modify communications, rewrite history, and ultimately change how people think and act. Their goal is control over the world's information supply. (If it's not, well then they've done a good job of stumbling into it on accident, haven't they?) There is no forgiveness or humanity here, just a ruthless authority staring over your shoulder at everything you say and do, ready to pounce.

You have many great options and zero more excuses. I will spend all my free time and money to help you, individually, dear reader. Hope to hear from you soon.

item.title: Gemini Time!
item.description:
Attention: you should be using Gemini.

I think the indie web space is pretty cool, if you do it right, but the tempatation to make anything but a static website is very strong. Most people don't do it right. Web bloat - JavaScript frameworks that bring in piles of dependencies with security issues, style sheets that only work in Chrome, dynamic content that takes forever to load, tracking, ads, and all sorts of other wastes of bandwidth and electricity - these are all status quo on the modern web. Want to use an older computer? Sorry, your average news or recipe website will bring it to its knees. These sites should just serve text, and maybe an image or two, but they do not.

Why a new protocol?

Essentially, nobody but hyper-nerds avoids web bloat. Web bloat is making us throw away perfectly usable computers, create e-waste, killing our planet with useless energy consumption, giving power to massive surveillance corpos, and recklessly introducing security problems left and right. Web bloat is morally wrong. The Web worked fine without JS and CSS frameworks. It can work that way again. It should. But now that this is the status quo, companies won't give it up - turning what should be text-only sites into interactive web applications that track your usage and location and battery level and heartbeat and GPS location is too tempting to most webmasters, assisted of course by marketing departements. You almost never see web bloat on a regular blog or personal website.

With a new protocol that is intentionally limited, temptation is avoided. Terminal-based browsers are a first-class citizen. Text is downloaded near-instantly. There's no energy waste because 100% of the page's content is, well, actual content, not literal junk that nobody wants.

I've had a gemini capsule (that's what we call websites) set up on this server for a while: (gemini link). You will need a special browser to use it. I like Amfora, but if you must have a GUI, Lagrange is also fantastic.

The Gemini space is like what the web was like in the early days. Before powerful computers, before smartphones. It's beautiful and interesting and full of people - not bloat.

item.title: On Freedom
item.description: I've written a bit about Freedom. You can find it in the Articles section. It's taken me a while to put it to words and it is still quite a bit of a ramble. If you read it thinking about computers or religion or homeownerhsip or exercise, it makes at least some sense.

item.title: Revisiting
item.description:
A blog post I saw today reminded me that the website it's on exists. This website happens to be a website I heavily referenced when first replacing the BIOS on my Thinkpad X220. It was a little over five years ago.

Much has changed in my life since then. I still have the X220, though it's been replaced (by another Coreboot-powered machine). I still use most of the software I started using around that time (except for X/i3 -> Wayland/Sway), and my usage of stable, predictable console tools has only increased. It started with the X220. I was a Linux user for a long while before I went all in on CLI-only and tiling window managers, and I'm far happier using an environment that's "mine". The meme-worthy "Thinkpad + [core/libre]boot + Arch Linux + tiling window manager" combination is actually quite comfy and Tyler's blog set me on the path.

The whole "I installed Arch Linux and now I really understand how my system works" trope is only kind of untrue. Understanding commands and components that go into building a usable desktop really helped me get a grasp of what I was working with. I had to know about, install, and configure video drivers, console configuration, bootloaders, the initramfs, disk partitioning, text editors, networking, sudo, systemd, audio, and a GUI, to name a few. Configuring these got me much more comfortable with command line editors and tmux. Rescuing a system from a live CD and doing the whole bind mounts of /proc and /sys wasn't useless - it helped me grow. Editing config files to modify my desktop was intially scary, but I am now very comfortable looking at config files, finding documentation, using man, and being able to troublshoot systems. This has not only helped me customize my desktop, but also really influenced the direction of my career and helped me land some sweet jobs.

To think it all started because I saw a thinpad and a cool window manager setup somewhere on the Internet, and thought to myself, "that's really cool! I want that!" I now use software that's stable, predictable, scriptable, and heavily configurable. Most of what I do on my computer is on the command line, in a terminal, and that journey because of some cool screenshots. I'm quite glad I fell for this meme.

Cool desktops don't change, they adapt and shift only when the user needs them to. Now, and for the past few years, that's my desktop, too. And that's pretty cool.
Thanks Tyler!

item.title: Ownership
item.description:
I've lived in an apartment for 2 months now, after living in an owned home the last few years. I now know I vastly prefer the latter, as well as running Linux, self-hosting, and not using a smartphone. Here's what I like about ownership.

When I own something, I am in control. For better or for worse, I have both the ownership and responsibility. If something breaks, I can and must fix it. When I rent something, I must wait for the real owner to fix it, and they often take longer and do a worse job than I would. At a job of mine when we hosted our own email server, it almost never went down, and if it did, it was quick to get back up or fail over, because of our high redundancy, low complexity, and complete ownership. O365, AWS, GCP, and others went down a few times, for multiple hours at a time, during that 2 year period. Our uptime was better because we had an owned system that was less complex.

If I want to change something, I can. Simple as that. Our cabinets here don't have handles and my work Macbook doesn't have a competent window manager. I would change both, if I could.

Things aren't changed out from under my feet when I own something. Ownership is stable and predictable. Rent will increase, user interfaces will change, APIs will go away, the owners might decide to shut the place down or turn off the service - I live at the whims of others. While I own the movies on my NAS and hard drive, but I've had streaming or digital "purchases" take my stuff away before. There are plenty of people running KDE3 and GNOME2 (Trinity, MATE) because they forked the old versions they liked and kept them around. Imagine if you could keep, customize, and update Windows 98 or iOS 6 yourself.

Ownership is harder. There's more stuff you have to deal with that most would rather not. But I like stability, privacy, security, and living on my terms, not the terms of some arbitrary master who may not share my values. Unless you own your stuff, use open protocols, and make sure you are in control of as much as you can be, you will spend your whole life getting the rug pulled out from under you. That's frustrating!

Living in this awful apartment has whiplashed me so hard that I want to start hosting my own email.*

*For the non-technical: self-hosting email is not generally considered a good idea on a small scale or individual basis, because the potential risks are too high for something as critical to modern life as email. It is a siren's song for sysadmins, both green and seasoned alike, as controlling one's own email does offer a great deal of control over personal data.

item.title: SXMO - Peak Linux User
item.description:
It was only a matter of time.

Back in February, I gave sxmo a serious try for the first time. I didn't quite get it and it didn't really "click" for me. I'd tried it before, because I've tried every postmarketOS interface at some point, but this was the first time I really truly gave it a go.

You know where this story ends. Like everything good in *NIX-land, it has a high initial learning curve, but is more lightweight, customizable, and ultimately, more user-friendly by virtue of being hackable. I use sway on the desktop, and now I'm using it on my phone, too. I don't have to understand how to use two different interfaces, becasue they're the same! It's like the people that use an iPhone and an iPad-as-laptop - it's the same interface between multiple devices.

Tiling window managers can actually make a lot of sense on smaller screens:

  • window decorations usually end up being smaller or nonexistent
  • it's easy to add in customizable launchers
  • scripting is a "first-class citizen", leading to less actions per task (even Apple thinks it's important)
  • window managers are lighter weight than a full "desktop" environment, leading to longer battery life
(This is only kind of a joke.)

A tiling window manager on a phone is peak Linux ricer. It's unironically hilarious. But also, seriously, this is the most fun I've had in a while. Being able to ssh into my phone and control its functions from an ncurses-like interface is incredible. Using the buttons in a different way, contextually, is also really cool. Before sxmo, the keyboard case was only kind of okay, but with sxmo, the keyboard case actually makes a lot of sense. This is a really natural way to use the pinephone - why else would one have this thing but not for hacking around in a totally weird Linux environment?

There are, of course, a few changes I've had to make to the environment, shortcuts, hooks, libraries and programs I've compiled from source and stuffed into my ~/.config directory... but that's what this was designed for. It's why I bought a Linux Phone. I'm really happy to be able to treat my telephone like a computer, instead of an appliance. Every aspect is a hacker's dream come true.

item.title: Why I Write Off Certain Types Of Music
item.description:
I figured out why I don't like country or most rap.

A brief sample of what I do listen to: classical music, jazz, good rock & roll, soundtracks, alternative rock from the 90's and 2000s (and yes, that "lo-fi" ambient music that's trendy these days; I happen to think it's good pleasant background music to work to). Feel free to write off my opinions now before you go any further!

I like this music because it stands alone. It's just music, and it sounds good, or it doesn't. It doesn't say "look at how cool I the singer am" and it doesn't require you to buy into the validity or primacy of a specific lifestyle. Rap is frequently about "look at me and how cool and rich I am, and let me describe for you my sexual prowess and/or how many people I've killed". Country's basically the same, but instead of flashy gold necklaces, fancy cars, and expensive booze it's a different set of objects (jeans and cowboy hats, tractors and old trucks, cheap beer), the descriptions of sex are much more subtle, and the violence is non-lethal.

They're both all about a specific lifestyle and how it's cool, instead of, you know, the music. It's an advertisement, basically.

If you haven't figured it out yet, I'm a fan of meritocracy. Anyway, that's it. Happy Saturday.

item.title: I Reinstalled Fedora Without Losing Data
item.description:
Yesterday I broke NetworkManager, and was reminded of the power of a separate /home partition.

It didn't recognize any devices - WiFi or ethernet. Reinstalling packages from downloads after wiping out configuration didn't seem to work either. I didn't want to spend much time figuring out why, because NetworkManager is a useful tool but highly complex house of cards, and because I'm a tired, busy man these days. I think it had something to do with rfkill, and the hardware WiFi switch on the computer.

Fedora defaults to the following layout (since about 33?):
/boot (1GB ext4)
/efi (512MB fat32, if you are EFI)
/ (rest-of-disk BTRFS volume)
/home (rest-of-disk BTRFS subvolume)
And I used this to my advantage! You can reinstall Fedora on / while leaving /home alone.

How I did it:
In the Fedora installer, I did custom partitioning (but not 'advanced custom'), decrypted my volume, selected to reformat the primary volume as /, and then told it to mount my /home at /home, but not reformat it. I also chose to reformat /boot. (The computer in question is not EFI).

I've never had to do this before, but knew it was theoretically possible. A quick dnf list --installed > packages.txt before reinstall helped me get back up and running quickly. And of course I had a full backup of my data should everything have gone south. At the end of it all, I had reinstalled my OS, reinstalled all my preferred programs, and retained all my data. The whole process took about an hour. Some of my manual packages I will have to rebuild, but I expected that anyway, and should have just put them in copr in the first place.

Lessons here:

  1. Always install with a separate /home partition
  2. Always keep install media around
  3. Keep backups so you're not afraid to try something new
  4. NetworkManager is great when it works but don't make it angry!
The end.

item.title: Responsibility, Availability, and Free Software
item.description:
I'm conflicted, and want to ask for advice.

But first, two short anecdotes.

  • When I was in college, I was available and reachable to my roommate. He woke me up late at night. His girlfriend's sister was sick and needed a ride to the hospital. I had a car. Since I was available, right there in his home with him, he asked me, and I gave him my keys. This enabled him to do something good for his girlfriend (now wife), and I was helpful and useful because I was available.
  • Shortly after college, I got a text message late in the evening from a friend. Because I was reachable, and available, I was able to respond to her need quickly and with love and attention. Now we're married.
Because I was reachable and available to my friends, I was able to affect positive change in my life, or help a friend out. I think that it is important to be reachable and available to one's friends.

Some definitions are required for me to be properly understood.
available: being immediately and easily able to freely give of one's time. Example: "Mary has a flexible job and takes every break she can to go out with her friends. She is always available."
reachable: being able to receive and respond to requests in a timely fashion. Example: "Colin answers his phone on the first ring, and responds to emails within the hour. He's very reachable."

I have a full-time job and a family. I have some hobbies and projects that I enjoy. I'm not always available but I should not be difficult to reach. I stay at home most of the time, have a good Internet connection, and have a lot of computers that are frequently on. It shouldn't be hard to get open a communication channel. There are a lot of ways to contact me.

Should I have a preference on how people should contact me with computers?
Should I, as I have in the past, enforce that preference?

But I worry about this a lot. I worry that my extreme preference for Free Software is (funnily enough) excluding people from being able to interact with me. Now, that doesn't make a lot of sense to me, because if I insist on Free Software, no one should be prohibited from contacting me. The only barrier is convenience. If the person on the other end for whatever reason refuses Freedom, I guess there's nothing I can do about it.

However, "don't make perfect the enemy of good" - if something is good enough, I should let it exist and work. I shouldn't put a bunch of silly overly-technical flaming hoops up for people to jump through, just to call me on the phone. It seems to confuse people that I actually do care about how they contact me. I just want it to be safe and reliable - unfortunately that's kind of a hard problem to solve these days.

All this to say, I want to use the weird computer stuff I want to use, because I'm a guy who likes weird computers. However, I use communication solutions that are, by their very nature, available to anyone and everyone, regardless of what they use. I don't put up barriers. I don't force people to do unethical things. It might be slightly more difficult than laying on the couch, eating chips, and channel surfing, but come on, you're better than that anyway.

Anyway. That's about it. I want to be reachable, because I am available. I want to be friendly and helpful and reliable to my friends and family. I believe that I am, and that enforcing my communication channels to be good things, but not necessarily easy, is kind of what I'm here for. Why even use a computer to talk to me if you're not interested in that sort of thing?

item.title: When Normal is Not Normal
item.description:
When people define the normal usage of computers and computing technology, they usually mean specific things - smartphones, appliance computers, web browsers, office suites, and the like. Nothing remotely complicated or, in their eyes, abnormal.

But these are computers. Programmable machines that calculate and compute. The coolest thing about computers is that they can be programmed and their behavior changed at will. Even so, that's not on the normie-approved list of what to do with a computer.

The most normal use of a computer is programming the computer.

What else are they here for? They're here for users to make them do arbitrary things, to write code and run it, and to hack and tinker and change the system at will. Using a computer with a half dozen high-rez cameras, a sensor array, multiple wireless communication modules, an enormous color display, and more compute power than existed in the whole world in 1980, all just to look at pictures of food, or view advertisements, or waste enormous amounts of energy on a communication protocol over 20 years old - that is truly odd.

A computer that can't be programmed misses the point of computers. The ability to change what the system does is what makes it so desirable. Using a computer without modifying it is insane behavior. Personal Computers are devices that can be modified to suit the needs of the user. One size does not fit all. It is our differences and uniqueness that make us interesting. We can't all be put in one box. So, reader, if you must use a computer, make it work for you. Program it. Hack it. Tinker and modify.
Use it normally.

item.title: Linux Mobile Setup
item.description:
This is a current snapshot of how I set up my mobile Linux system (Pine64 Pinephone, as of February 2022) for future reference and to help me collate all my information in one place. I usually forget something when I upgrade or reinstall the device.

I create my OS image with pmbootstrap. pmbootstrap offers a lot of choices. I choose edge, phosh, and set my locale to en_US.UTF-8, as God intended. I have a 2GB MicroSD card running JumpDrive to install PostmarketOS to the eMMC of the Pinephone with --sdcard=/dev/sda --fde.

After the system boots, I join it to the network, and install the following:
vim git htop rsync mpv ffmpeg urlscan newsboat phosh-antispam vvmplayer dino flatpak gnome-podcasts flatpak numberstation yad seahorse powersupply geary evolution foot dconf-editor yad
Next I add add flathub and my repo to flatpak remotes. I install my builds of Signal, Axolotl, and my Dice program (all detailed on my repo's site) and NhekoReborn, FluffyChat, KeePassXC, and Komikku. I install KeePassXC on from flatpak because it keeps QT libraries off the main system, and I'm installing them with NhekoReborn anyway.

I run the restore outlined in the "upgrade" detailed above from my encrypted "backup" sdcard, which I regularly rsync --delete my home directory to.

On PostmarketOS, I add the following cron entries:

# daytime
30 06 * * * /home/adam/bin/profile-swap restore
30 07 * * * /home/adam/bin/themeswitch.sh light
# nighttime
59 19 * * * /home/adam/bin/profile-swap store
59 19 * * * /home/adam/bin/profile-swap silent
30 19 * * * /home/adam/bin/themeswitch.sh dark
And speaking of cron, I use rc-update to add crond and waked to the default runlevel. Waked ensures the clock program will wake up the phone and sound the alarm when appropriate.

The keyboard case is fun. It's kind of broken and not really ideal - I'd like to to be thinner and larger - but it's awesome to have when on the go. At home I mostly don't use it because I can't plug the phone into my dock when the case is attached - power from 2 sources at once is bad. When using the keyboard I change the scaling from 200% to 150% or 175%. Phosh doesn't work well in landscape at all.

And that's about it. Future self, hope this helps.

item.title: Pinephone February 2022
item.description:
How's the Pinephone thing going? Glad you asked, nobody!

I write this from convergence land. Recent updates have transformed this thing from "okay-ish" to "surprisingly usable"! My phone-computer is plugged into my monitor, and I am typing with external perhipherals. It's pretty sweet. There has been an enormous amount of work by many others (and a tiny amount by yours truly!) to get this thing to a workable state. I imagine the Librem 5 or Pinephone Pro would be even better, but this is what I've got. It's still incredible.

My main SIM card lives in the Pinephone most of the time. I occasionally swap back to my Android device, but try to avoid using it otherwise. It is faster, has a working camera, calls sound clear due to HD Voice, and it can run Signal natively, but I don't like it nearly as much. The Pinephone is what I want to use.

On a daily basis, the phone does very few things when undocked - it needs to do calls, messages, music, podcasts, and maybe some email? That's about it. When docked, I treat it like a full computer. The details of my personal software stack are as follows:

  • It runs PostmarketOS Edge with the Phosh UI. I've found this to be the most "feature complete" OS that requires the least amount of hackery on my part. I used to run Arch and that worked fine too, just a little less usable, and I had to compile a lot of packages and features that PostmarketOS just has natively.
  • Chatty is for XMPP (with omemo) and SMS/MMS. It works well!
  • Dino for XMPP as a secondary client.
  • Geary and Evolution for email.
  • NhekoReborn (Flatpak) for Matrix.
  • KeepassXC with scale-to-fit for password management. It is far better than gnome-passwordsafe/"Secrets" due to the insanely high resource usage of the latter.
  • GNOME Podcasts to listen to podcasts.
  • Lollypop is for music.
  • Signal-Desktop flatpak (built from these excellent arm64 signal builds).

The battery life isn't great. I await my keyboard case anxiously. It can't really do the whole "portable hotspot" thing, but that can be easy enough to figure out.

I'm satisfied with this for now. It's not great but it is incredibly cool and useful having a full computer in my pocket. It's what I dreamed smartphones could be, but never were.

item.title: Breaking Changes
item.description:
Breaking Changes are always interesting to deal with. Recently I've been trying to get a few bugs fixed in software I used, and build the resulting software. I use GNOME Podcasts on my Pinephone, and it used to have a pretty annoying bug where it would download the album art every time you viewed the media widget in the notification panel or lock screen. This was the byproduct of several bugs, both in phosh, and in gnome-podcasts. The bugs have been fixed, but it's taking a while for them to move down the pipe from git master, to release, to distributions. Sometimes, you just have to take matters into your own hands.

So a-building we will go. I really should get another Raspberry Pi to use as an arm64 build server, but until then I'll continue using the free ARM server on Oracle Cloud's Free Tier. I am not a fan of Oracle the company but they do offer a pretty nice set of resources for free, which includes ARM resources. (4 cores, 24 GB RAM, combine-able or divisible into up to 4 individual systems.) I've got the ARM resources set up just as one server with all the resources running Ubuntu.

In order to get an interactive Arch setup, I run Docker on the system, managed with Distrobox. I use the menci/archlinuxarm base image, and just exec into that. Distrobox automatically shares the home directory transparently with the container, so all the files I need are accesible both from the host system and the Arch Linux ARM container. The setup isn't automated or anything. I just write PKGBUILDs and run makepkg. Pretty primitive, but most of the software I am building has a pretty hacky, beta, manual setup anyway.

Anyway, all this to say, Meson broke on everyone recently! (issue 1, issue 2) GNOME Podcasts, like pretty much all moder GNOME/Linux Desktop software, uses Meson. It's a system I've been meaning to learn for a while but have basically zero understanding of. Browsing the issues, I figured out that I needed to imitate some of the other projects, and remove some declarations Meson's Makefile equivalent in order to get GNOME Podcasts to build:

sed -e "s/i18n.merge_file (.*$/i18n.merge_file (/" -i podcasts-$pkgver/podcasts-gtk/resources/meson.build

And now I'm a happy GNOME Podcast listener. I learned a few new things from this experience. Upstream changes happen super fast; Arch Linux doesn't always have the latest stuff (glibc on Fedora is newer!); and when you're living on the bleeding edge of software releases, waiting on critical issues and hardware support, releases seem to come really slowly!

FOSS is great because I could look at public code and public issues, figure out how to build my Podcast app myself, fix the build, install it, and use it. Thanks to all the GNOME and Meson people who figured this out before I did. I can't get over how cool it is to have a smartphone I can do this with.

item.title: Better Might Not Be Ready
item.description:
or, you might not be ready for it

Recently a high-profile Internet Elder called the concept of decentralized financial systems a bad idea, mainly because of energy consumption, and because people who got into it early had a lot of money, and those who didn't care about it had none. You might not understand what I'm talking about - that's okay. This instance isn't important, but what is important is that he misses the point by a mile and a half, and is using his weight to publicly discourage improvement of a potentially better system because it isn't ready yet.

Bitcoin and Monero are good, sound, stable cryptocurrencies that make the world better. Cryptocurrency does consume a lot of energy, sure, but there are a lot of things that do, and good ideas should hardly be the first on the chopping block. The first should be the yearly smartphone and chip cycle - release a new one every 3 or 4 years, and instead of throwing the old stuff away, unlock it and open source the code, so instead of throwing batteries and metals in landfills, we can throw away planned obsolecense! If we're dead-set on decreasing the energy consumption of financial systems, why turn off the good one? How about we turn off MasterCard or Visa instead? Maybe the stock market, and betting on the fate of companies? That churns through electricity, and lots of people get hurt by it, and by the endless purusit of year-over-year growth.

The point is this:
Bitcoin and Monero are better than whatever currency your government's using. They belong to no entity but the people that use them. You can't make more. You can't cheat or steal or lie about what's going on. These systems put the power back into the hands of the people, and take it away from adversarial and oppressive organizations that want to control and change the system on their whims. The Linux Desktop is better than Windows and macOS. It supports mutiple architectures, brings decade-old computers back to business, doesn't exfiltrate your data to the NSA, and generally give the user more choice and customization than the other proprietary systems. It too gives the power to the user, making them responsible, but free.

But they aren't quite ready yet.

Photographers need Photoshop, architects need AutoCAD and SolidWorks, organizations need Active Directory support, and most people just buy a computer in a retail store and use what it comes with. There are still plenty of problems with using a Linux Desktop, though I've been exclusively using it for almost five years, and doing everything but gaming there for about ten. Although it's ready for most people, there are still plenty for whom it's not accessible - either beause of actual accessibility concerns, or just lack of knowledge.

Cryptocurrencies are the same. Just like you don't need 10,000 different choices of Linux Distribution (Hannah Montana Linux, RebeccaBlackOS, Biebian Linux, and not-Linux PonyOS are all real computer operating systems you can use!) you don't need DogeCoin or other stupid meme cryptocurrencies. Not every United State needs to have their own coin, and neither do messaging apps. At some point, we all settled on a standard for communication, and that's email. Eventually one or a few cryptocurrencies will "win" and they'll be the ones people take seriously - seems like Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Monero are all the ones that the DeFi and Privacy people can agree on, so maybe them?

Anyway, point is, maybe the things that are better don't quite hit the mark for being as functionally complete as we want yet. But that doesn't mean that the idea sucks or it's a monstrosity that's destroying lives. The idea is better, but the implementation just needs to be ironed out. Bitcoin consumes a lot of power, but Monero's algorithm is ASIC resistant and can be mined on normal CPUs, making it something that's far more attainable for regular folks. Linux is truly Free (as in freedom), but in order to live in the better brighter freer future, we need more better programs to work on Linux and other FOSS operating systems.

We have a glimpse of the future with us today, in the form of FOSS and Decentralized tech, but we still need to help it along. Get comfortable with it, use it a bunch, figure out what's wrong, and fix it! Giving up and throwing in the towel is wrong. We won't get a better world like that. Sometimes it sucks to be an early adopter, but you don't have to go all in - experiment, play around, report bugs, and get more people thinking about how to fix the problems. It's slow going but Rome wasn't built in a day. No need to use something in production that doesn't suit your needs, but don't denigrate it as a failure when it's clearly built to last. The future's just getting started.

item.title: Check Out GNOME Flashback
item.description:
In the Linux/FOSS world, GNOME seems to be the target of a lot of ire, both with and without good reason. The people that work on GNOME stuff tend to be pretty opinionated and stubborn, but the stuff that they make is also some of the best Free Software around. The GNOME desktop environment works well, rarely had issues, and is quite user friendly.

But we all know that GNOME is pretty good already (if not a bit resource heavy). Today, I want to talk about GNOME Flashback. The Flashback project is a tiny set of programs that you can install next to a traditional GNOME desktop that bring back the old look of GNOME 2. It's not like the GNOME Classic session which is just some GNOME 3 extensions - it's almost a totally different desktop environment, like MATE, but uses all the stuff you've already got installed on a traditional GNOME session. It's a grand total of 10 new packages on top of a Fedora Workstation install. It still uses GNOME control center and GNOME tweaks to change settings (like adding minimize/maximize buttons, and changing keyboard shortcuts) but otherwise behaves like a separate entity.

You may know that I use and enjoy sway, a customizable tiling window manager, but that's certainly not for everyone, or even every application. I've spent a lot of time in my ~/.sway/config, adding various programs to autostart and tuning settings. I and others have attempted to add a bunch of stuff to sway to make it into a proper DE, but that doesn't suit a lot of workflows or people. GNOME Flashback can get you most of the way there - it is very low on resource utilization, integrates well with GNOME services like keychain and polkit, so password saving and authentication prompts work well, and keyboard shortcuts are configurable through GNOME control center.

GNOME Flashback Session, configured to my liking

The Arch Wiki page on GNOME Flashback is, of course, quite helpful. Initially figuring out the configuration of the panels (Super+Alt+Right-click?) isn't very discoverable, but that's about the only rough spot. I've configured it to be pretty close to what I consider ideal - some simple small menus, dmenu, and keyboard shortcuts. And, of course, Flashback is an Xorg session, so fix screen tearing on your computer first.

I've really been liking GNOME Flashback for the past couple days. It's easy to install next to your existing GNOME setup, doesn't mess with the system like a different DE to a GNOME setup would, integrates very easily, and is easy pick up. If you use GNOME already, try it out; it's available in Debian, Fedora, and Arch. It's a classic, less flashy, lighter weight version of what you're already using.

item.title: Everything Works on the Pinephone
item.description:
What happened?

As of today, I got phone calls, data, SMS, MMS, and reliable Internet communication working on the Pinephone. That means everything that one would reasonably expect a smartphone to do, this thing does it. (If you don't know what the Pinephone is, it's a Desktop Linux computer in the size and shape of a smartphone.) I am attempting to switch to using a Linux computer as my personal smartphone.

Why should you care?

I've explained my dissatisfaction with Signal before so I won't rehash it, but as of today, Signal is now a low-priority communication platform for me. I will not be immediately responsive there as I have been in the past.

How can you get in touch with me?

If you want to get ahold of me:

  1. Chat with XMPP - address is just my email address
  2. Email me
  3. Call me
  4. Matrix (email me if you are curious)
Do not send me SMS or MMS messages. Really. Don't. Email me instead.

Why?

I don't like smartphones. This "smartphone" device respects me. It lets me do what I want with it. It's as simple, or complicated, as I want it to be.

item.title: Old Laptop Hardware Rocks
item.description:
Some old-ish machines, opened up

I love old laptops. I like pretty much all old computers, because they're a snapshot in time of where we were, and it helps me realize how far things have come. (Or helps learn lessons about how far things have regressed.) But I especially enjoy old laptops, because laptops, in particular, have always been really interesting computers.

Inside each laptop is a unique solution to the same problem; the problem being of course "how do we cram all this in there and make it work safely". Different manufacturers (and even different models) can take a different approach to the same problems. Some open through the bottom, and some open on the top, making the keyboard an integral part of the disassembly. Some have hard disks externally accessible without full disassembly. Some pack every component on to the motherboard, while others modularize with ribbon cables and daughterboards. Some had many cute little doors for individual components, like the RAM or hard disk, while keeping the rest of the system locked away in a plastic coffin.

I like to think I've learned a bit from observing many machines of different vintages over the last however-many years I've been tearing apart laptops. What follows are some observations and "lessons".

Long ago, RAM was stacked, which made machines thicker. Then it became side-by-side, which took more space, then manufacturers only provided one SO-DIMM slot, then it became soldered. Batteries going internal helped save more space, the removal of the DVD drive more still. Many components have gotten smaller, but really all that means is laptops have gotten thinner and more fragile. I bet most folks wouldn't notice an extra few MM of thickness put toward device sturdiness.

Newer machines have a lot less empty space in them. Connecters aren't standing up, they're lying flat. Things are not multi-layered anymore. Those little doors for individual components often made other parts way harder to get at. A consequence of this is that newer machines tend to use the "whole bottom panel" strategy: 9-12 screws and the bottom cover of a machine is off, and you can access everything. I like this better.

Lots of Dell machines prioritized being able to remove the hard drive without removing the bottom panel. Smart. It was in its own slot, but it wasn't a "manylittle doors" situation (as above). This was really cool.

Captive screws are a godsend and plastic clips are an abomination. They will eventually break. Metal chassis stand the test of time.

If you want to see how repairable a laptop really is, see how many power connectors it has (USB-C has made this a little better), and see if they can be replaced individually. When a power connector gets loose or breaks, can you replace it for $15, or do you need to replace the whole darn motherboard, soldered CPU and all? Newer machines are not nearly as good at this.

Keyboards are now almost impossible to replace. On older machines, they often came off with very little fuss, and now it's either they have to come out last, after the battery and motherboard, or they don't come out at all.

Older, thicker fans made lower pitched noise, which sounds quieter. Older machines had way more space to put coolers, too. A modern 15W CPU will spin its thin whiny screamer of a fan more often than an older 35W laptop chip. Energy efficiency is cool, and it's led to fanless laptops, but fans have gotten worse. We're in a "middle period". Performance kind of stagnated because of the drop in power consumption and we're finally past it, but we're ripe for another efficiency gain. Soon all laptops should be fanless, or rarely use fans.

Models that get cute and creative with component placement are my favorite. The Lenovo X220/X230 palmrest is a super fun thing to open. The T430 has one SO-DIMM under the keyboard and one under the door on the bottom, and its Wi-Fi card underneath the keyboard. The HP in the picture has a swing-out smartcard reader resting with rubber feet on top of the hard drive, and the Dell keeps its Bluetooth card right under a little plastic piece by the hinge. It's so fun to think about how these things happened, and who made the decisions!

Socketed CPUs in laptops were awesome and they didn't have to go away. The last system I have with a socket isn't much thicker than one with a soldered CPU from the same year, and when laptops got thinner than those, they lost Ethernet and decent cooling anyway. Not worth it.

Vertical USB ports were super cool. Vertical fans were not, just funny. MXM cards and other weirdo daughterboards are also hilarious, but in a bad way.

I once took a pile of four Dell E6440 laptops, all with broken components, and made two working systems. It is my favorite laptop I've ever opened up because I didn't break anything during the surgeries, and all the components were really easy to get at and very understandable. HP EliteBooks from the same era come in second because of how they label screws. That's awesome!

Anyway. Kind of stream-of-consciousness, but I like disassembling these systems and seeing how they were put together. If you're a laptop maker or know one, please email me! I'd love to talk to you about your hardware and why you made the decisions you did. That's all. Have a good evening.

item.title: New Article On Dumbphones
item.description:
New article about dumbphones in the articles section. This is my least autistic ramble yet. I will update it if I get more info so keep on the lookout.

You could just read it, but the article is about switching from smartphones to a "dumb" phone, how to do it well, and what not to do. I've heard a lot about how this is sometimes a more popular desire than getting a GrapheneOS-level secure, de-Googled smartphone, so figured I'd fill that need.

item.title: Help For Those Who Want It
item.description:
For much of my life I've been unofficially doing "technology consulting" for my friends, family, classmates, neighbors, and really anyone who asks. Anything from ripping CDs and DVDs and converting home movies, or wiring a sound system, to building PCs, providing purchasing advice, fixing software problems, and setting up a network-wide ad blocker in someone's home. All of this I have done for free, for people I know. I don't go out seeking people who need help or advertising myself, but I volunteer and make myself available for those in my circle.

Over time, I've learned that there are two types of assistance I can provide:

  1. Fixing problems with people's goods
  2. Filling gaps in people's knowledge

What I really want is to help people interested in improving their own knowledge. I want to make people smarter, not make people's stuff less broken until they break it again, because they've learned nothing. I would rather be a teacher, than a mechanic. Of course, sometimes it's a bit of both, but I prefer Number 2 - it's more empowering for the individual!

I will, as before, continue doing this stuff for free in my spare time, because I belive it makes the world a better place, I enjoy it, and I already have a full-time job that pays well enough. But I'm trying to make it more of a formal experience - I will write up an outline and a curriculum with what I'm willing and able to teach and start putting it into practice when I am asked to help.

With all that out of the way, here are some basic questions I propose, as a kind of advertisement.

  • Are you happy with your computers? (Phones, printers, Wi-Fi routers, those count too)
  • Do they do everything you want them to, and nothing you don't?
  • Are you concerned about the behavior of the companies that control your digital experience?
  • Do you think digital privacy, safety, and security are important?
  • Are you happy with how you interact with the Internet, and how it interacts with you?
  • Do you feel like you have to spend too much money on computers?
  • Are you a bit creeped out by computers sometimes?
  • Do you have questions about computers, but don't know who to trust, and where to start?

I can answer your quetions and solve your problems. I will help you with purchasing decisions, help you get more life out of your existing equipment, help you gain back some privacy and security, and in general make your digital life easier to understand, safer, and more free. There's a spectrum to everything, and we can take it as far as you want, depending on how much time and interest you have. Email me. Let's chat. I'd love to hear from you. I want to help you help yourself.

item.title: A Note on VPNs
item.description:
VPN (Virtual Private Network) technology was created for the purpose of bridging multiple networks together. It allows remote workers to work on a secure work network, enables access to resources behind firewall or NAT, and allows servers to talk to each other like they're in the same room, even if they're miles away from each other. It's not a technology most regular computer users should have to think about.

If advertising has tricked you into thinking VPNs are cool, watch this video.

If you don't have time for that, here are some facts:

  • VPNs do not provide better encryption than HTTPS, so most of the time, using public WiFi isn't a problem, like it was a decade ago.
  • Your browsing history still lives on your computer, which is likely already backdoored by MS/Apple/Google. VPNs do nothing to protect your browsing history.
  • "No-log" VPN providers still log stuff if they're asked.
  • You will still be tracked across the Internet if you log into anything like email or social media.
  • You will still be tracked across the Internet by other means than your IP Address. Your IP is low on the totem pole of the long list of stuff that is used to track specifically, uniquely, you.
  • If you want to bypass geo-restricted content, use a proxy. This is better than funnelling ALL your traffic over the VPN's network. Just pass what you need through the tunnel. They're cheap to operate and cost less than a VPN.
  • If you need a VPN for work, cool. Use it.
  • If you VPN back to your home to access your home servers, that's a good use case.
  • If you are concerned about your ISP seeing what you connect to, use an alternate DNS server.
  • If you're even more concerned, use Tor (via Tor Browser) or a proxy.
  • If your Internet activity is questionably legal or illegal, or your ISP is actively malicious, use Tor with bridges or a proxy.
  • If you want to pirate stuff, think about your life for a bit.

There's almost no reason for most people to use a VPN. If you really think you need one, learn how to construct one in your home or in the cloud. Congrats, you spent less money and made yourself smarter.

item.title: Moving Back to XMPP
item.description:
First, for the impatient, from now on, please talk to me via XMPP - my ID is either my personal email address, or [firstname]@ath9k.com. Use Conversations on Android and Gajim or Dino on GNU/Linux. For other OSs, look here.

History Time

Over the past several years I've somewhat aggressively pushed everyone in my life to use Signal, since it's generally the easiest to use messenger program that checks most of my boxes. It is encrypted, open source, cross-platform, and has clients for desktop computers. Despite my belief that the cracks are beginning to show, Signal remains a safe, secure messenger that is available to everyone with an Apple iPhone (with associated iCloud account) or a Google Android Product (with associated Google account).

However, it doesn't fit the more important critera that I've been ignoring. The server is centralized, so I can't run my own if I so choose. The server software is only marginally open source (for what I belive is a stupid reason). The Signal team is openly hostile to third-party clients, will not support decentralization, the service requires a cell phone number for sign-up, and the desktop clients are dependent on the smartphone client. These are non-concerns for most people right now, but they always end up being the killer anti-features.

When a platform is centralized and does not use open standards, it will go away, and it will hurt when it does. Remember AIM? ICQ? Google Talk? When these systems shut down, they forced you to move to a new platform, regardless of if you were ready or not. When Signal inevitably does something else to close their system down, bakes in some boneheaded cryptocurrency scheme, or decides to remove support for de-Googled Android custom ROMs, what then? Can we use an old client, move our server, all without effecting our contacts? No dice. When Signal says jump, the users' only option is "how high".

If you wonder why I fret about chat client so much, it's because I want the primary way I communicate with friends and family to be safe, secure, and as personal as if we were face to face. I don't want anyone but me and the recipient to see the message. That's it. That's the one thing that's important. You have no idea how difficult it is to ensure that one requirement.

Personal Complaints

Besides the problems with the larger organization and system as a whole, I have personal issues with Signal.

  • They do not ship a decent desktop client. It's made of web junk and painful to run on my low-resource computers.
  • Despite being made of web technologies, they do not ship a "web" client like WhatsApp or Telegram, which would mitigate the following issue:
  • They only ship x86_64 builds of their desktop client, so users of the (now >year-old) M1 Macbooks, or any Windows or Linux ARM devices cannot use the desktop client. This also means no Pinephone client.
  • They do not officially support non-Debian Linux. Flatpaks and Snaps are unofficial builds by Internet randos. Building it from source is painful.
  • It's not possible to register without a phone number.
  • It's not possible to set up Signal on a new computer without "approving" it with the phone first. The desktop client can be used with the phone completely off, though.
  • Installing Signal without identifying yourself to Apple or Google is possible but highly discouraged. The Android standalone download is literally labeled "Danger Zone". They also refuse to distribute Signal via F-Droid, the good Android app store.
In short, it's heavily smartphone-dependent, which means it's also heavily Apple and Google dependent. How they ship an encrypted messenger and laud the benefits of privacy - while simultaneously ensuring their customers also have to submit to surveillance by these 2 tech giants in order to user their product - baffles me.

Where Do We Go?

There's a lot pushing me away from Signal. The only thing keeping me there is the same thing that keeps people on Facebook - the network effect. If Signal were decentralized like email, I could choose my own clients, run my own server, and solve the problem for myself, without effecting others. This is why I must flee to a decentralized messenger on an open standard - the next time I have complaints about what my messaging client does, I can swap it out and my recipients aren't inconvenienced. This is why I'm not telling everyone to switch to Telegram or WhatsApp - they have all the same issues Signal does, and more.

There are a bunch of options for decentralized, self-hosted messengers out there, but the one with staying power is XMPP (aka "jabber"). It has served as the backbone for a bunch of proprietary messengers, like Cisco Jabber and Google Talk, so it's battle-tested. There are many different clients and servers for every platform under the sun, and many are lightweight and resource friendly. The reason I don't want to migrate to Matrix is that it's too new, the one (!) server implementation is a resource hog, and out of all the clients, the only generally competent one is written in Electron, for a matching waste of energy on your server and client.

Using decentralized systems, or systems under your own control, mitigates the eventuality of proprietary or centralized systems turning sour. I'm running my own XMPP server on this webserver right now, using prosody. It is almost trivially easy to set up, can run on minimal system resources, and for extra security, SSL certs are free via LetsEncrypt. For those unwilling to go to the command line, YunoHost bundles it in the default setup.

Signal has been great. I've enjoyed using the program from its early days, but using what amounts to a proprietary system has restricted my freedoms long enough. I want to be able to run my own client. I want to be able to run my own server. I want to control my data. Signal affords me none of these freedoms. XMPP does. Some people see the availability of these choices as "fragmentation", but I see it as a good thing. What I want out of a chat program may not be what you want, but at the very least, our different clients should speak the same language.

I hope to see you there.

It's been about a year since I first got a Pinephone. Earlier this year I attempted to use it as my main smartphone and gave up after a week. Here's my progress report for October 2021.

Too Many OS Choices

There are a lot of choices for which operating system to use on the Pinephone. A non-exhaustive list can be found here. Instead of consolidating efforts, the makers have decided to build a bunch of different OSs and let users choose how they want their phone to be broken. Most of them are the same, with minor differences. If they were to be combined, it's possible that a franken-OS of the PMOS base, with the Manjaro tweaks, would be perfect. But that doesn't exist. I'd recommend choosing either one of those, for reason explained below.

I use Phosh since it's the best performing and usable UI on the Pinephone. Plasma Mobile is very slow, confusing, and crashy. SXMO is cool, but, it's DWM for the phone, so it takes a special kind to use it. I use a tiling window manager on the desktop, but a phone is different. I've tested the following operating systems in the last month:

  • Mobian - same state as last year, basically. Things are stable, solid, and mostly work as you'd expect. Nice, but almost no progress. Stasis. It still works, but everything else works better now because of the constant progress being made on this device, and its interface.
  • PostmarketOS - the most complete operating system. Things like alarm clock, not-fully-suspending-while-playing-media, and other various auto configs work out of the box. If you're just using the device in a stock state this is the best choice. This is where most of the innovation comes from that leaks out into other distros.
  • Arch Linux - like a more up to date Mobian. Some basic OS things don't work but it's fast, up to date, and rather stable. Very upstream-y, not an opinionated configuration.
  • Manjaro - I expected not to like it, but it is my favorite currently. It is the only one in this list that lacks full disk encryption support, which is kind of table stakes for a modern mobile OS, but it manages to be blazing fast, have a decent out of the box setup, and unlike x86 Manjaro, be an actually pleasant and useful set of additions on to Arch. The UI and look and feel of just using Phosh on Manjaro is noticeably fast. It's really slick.
So yeah, I'm using Manjaro on the eMMC, set up with all my regular phone stuff, but have PMOS on my SD card since it's also fun to play with.

Cell Phone

This thing apparently makes phone calls. The same things that worked a year ago still seem to work fine - phone calls and data are a zero-configuration deal. MMS is still spotty apparently, but I do not use it or SMS. This time, my SIM card was only in the Pinephone briefly to test features, so I can't speak to anything long term.

Messaging

Telegram works great, but Telegram is not for me. Lots of xmpp clients too, since this is the Linux Desktop. There is an abundance of Matrix clients. Fluffychat is quite nice, but very basic, and the interface elements are cartoonlishly large. Fractal is laggy but usable. Nheko is barely usable. Signal, the program I've spent 5 years begging everyone in my life to use, is conspicuously abset. There are a lot of efforts trying to get it working, but nothing is sufficient. Best effort is running it in Waydroid, but that's kind of awful.

Modern messaging apps that include calling, messaging, and video chat, like Signal, WhatsApp, Viber, FB Messenger, etc. - that's not what this device is good at. Clients for these services do not exist for the Pinephone and likely never will.

Misc

Email, calendar/contact syncing, all work marvelously with Evolution's data sync daemons. Many programs exist to do various other phone things, like password management, note taking, and all that. Apps lists can be found here, at linmobapps, or here, at the mobian wiki.

Android emulators like Waydroid or Anbox have been moderately useful for bridging the gap, for things like Signal that don't have native Linux apps. But it's hideously slow on the Pinephone. Network sometimes drops in the Android container. Like the Pinephone, something for testing, but don't depend on it.

Conclusions

The Pinephone is a great PDA. It's like a Palm Pilot - it runs a limited set of discrete applications, but isn't ready for me to use as my phone. If you have already designed most of your life, and your communication (that's key) around open standards like Matrix, xmpp, and email, then it can be a usable smartphone. Otherwise, stick with an Android ROM like Calyx, Graphene, or Lineage OS without Google Apps. Almost as FOSS, and more support for what we use phones for these days.

Someday it'll be perfect. Not today, though. I'm still gonna buy the keyboard when it comes out. That might push me over the edge. Here's to hoping.

item.title: People Want Crutches
item.description:
I'm not one for short term solutions (see here). Amongst my family I'm sometimes treated as the "go-to guy" for anything involving a plug. Almost without fail, I try a long-term fix every single time someone asks me for a solution. I try to "teach a man to fish", to borrow a turn of phrase.

Overwhelmingly, this does not work.

People think I'm talking in too complicated of terms, they think I'm ridiculing them, and usually throw their hands up and either abandon their quest, or just ask me to do it for them. The latter takes some humility, which I can respect, but usually I refuse these requests. I want everyone to be smart about computers. I want more people to talk with about a very important thing in my life. I would love it if I could install Gentoo with my mom. That would be awesome. But I would rather have someone abandon computers entirely, do paper billing, use a rotary phone, and live in a cabin in the woods, than get used to using computer-smart people as a crutch.

It's your stuff. Your PC, your Personal Computer is about you. I want you to have the freedom and the ownership. I want you to have the responsibility, but also the power. Computers are unlike anything else. They're highly integrated and personalized by their owner.

I understand the feeling of desperation when things aren't working out. I feel the same way when someone asks me to do something for them that I've tried to teach. I just want people to get better, not rely on me. But usually, I'm disappointed to find that people want crutches.

item.title: Why Use The Command Line?
item.description:
When modern, flexible, decent graphical interfaces exist, many people think there's little reason to use the command line; that it's difficult to use or obsolete. This couldn't be further from the truth. The command line is powerful, verbose, easy to understand, filled with help documents and tutorials, and conceptually incredibly simple. You ask, it answers. But the main reason to use it is because, most of the time, it's faster and easier than using a graphical interface.

The command line is not always the easiest way to do things, especially for novices. But it is the fastest way to do most things, and the only way to do some things.

Using the command line for touching up a photo isn't a great idea. If you have to print or change one document, sure, use the tools where you feel at home. But if you need to resize 100 photos, or print 100 documents, or make a small change to 100 text files? You bet you're gonna need the command line. That's robot work. Are you really gonna open and close 100 pictures in your image editor, or sift through your filesystem, looking for duplicate files to get back some space? The command line will rip through these tasks in a single second where a human would take hours to do it in a graphical interface. Make the computer automate it for you!

It's important to know how to use good tool. Read this tutorial if you want to know more. If you have a command line you can interface with, you have infinite power at your fingertips. Have fun. Email me if you want help.

item.title: In Praise of ath9k
item.description:
Do you have a favorite device driver? Or maybe a favorite pice of hardware? I do! My favorite hardware is the set of devices supported by Linux's ath9k (or OpenBSD's athn driver).

The devices in question are all Qualcomm Atheros wireless cards. They were in vogue from about 2011-2014 and shipped in a lot of consumer hardware. Most higher end notebooks shipped with Intel Wi-Fi chips, and the lower end ones shipped with Realtek or Broadcom cards. Atheros has less market share than the larger players in the "laptop Wi-Fi chip" space. This makes it somewhat complicated to find these cards in a reliable way, especially given that they mostly shipped in the mini-PCIe form factor, not M.2/NGFF. Most laptops these days also expect the Wi-Fi and Bluetooth chipsets to be on the same card in a combo pack, and those are also difficult to obtain reliably, since older systems with these cards frequently had a separate bluetooth module. Not only that, if you do find one with Bluetooth, it's likely it's older, and won't have modern Bluetooth features. Most of these cards also only have 2.4GHz bands for Wi-Fi and won't see 5GHz networks.

Despite the drawbacks, I go out of my way to find these cards. I've ordered a few on the Internet in various form factors and have them in every laptop I care about. Every machine I repair for a friend gets checked, and if it's got the Holy Grail of Wi-Fi, I ask to trade them for an Intel or other one of my spare cards, and since they run Windows it's usually an upgrade for them. In my extensive laptop experience, there is no Wi-Fi card that has worked better than the ath9k cards in terms of reliability, stability, connection speed, and versatility. Sometimes Intel cards dump their firmware all over the floor and leave a mess in dmesg and a reboot is necessary to clean up. Newer Qualcomm Atheros cards (ath10k) or Realtek cards either take a long time to connect or are single-band cards that can only handle one connection at a time, and in Realtek's case, that's if there's a driver at all! Sometimes their cards are such obscure one-offs that they don't land in the Linux kernel at all.

In honor of this driver and these wonderful cards, these blob-free bastions of Freedom, I made a website dedicated to these wireless cards and their driver. Enjoy. Go get yourself an Atheros card and bless your laptop with it to find out what you've been missing.

item.title: Discrete Tools
item.description:
I carry a Leatherman with me everywhere I go. It's nice to have a slightly-more-capable pocketknife on hand, in a pinch. In my garage, I use my "real" tools, since the Leatherman makes for a poor knife, lousy pliers, and a hardly serviceable screwdriver. But it does have them all.

In contrast to the above multi-tool, I also own a lot of small, cheap devices that I only really use for one thing each. While the wastefulness doesn't make me feel great, and the amount of space things take up can be a burden, I belive it makes for a healthier lifestyle. Allow me to explain.

When I'm reading a book on my e-reader device, a stray message, email, or update will not distract me, because that thing doesn't have that functionality. Listening to music or podcasts on my media player is the same - no potential distractions, because the device only does one thing. No matter how far smartphone cameras have come in the past few years, even a decade-old SLR will knock their socks off if the conditions are less than perfect - and you can't miss the perfect shot due to an incoming phone call. Turning off your alarm in the morning shouldn't also be an on-ramp right into the wailing and grinding of teeth that is social media. You deserve time to wake up.

A dedicated device forces you to be deliberate about your choices. While integration is nice, and I marveled at the utility of early smartphones as pocket computers, it just turns out they're bad at pictures, reading, gaming, playing music, watching videos, and email, but they can be bad at all of those things at once. They're built for distraction and task-thrashing and end up hurting more than they help. Cramming everything you do in a smartphone is pretty magical, but also a pretty dumb idea. I could pry open that case with the mini screwdriver on my multi-tool, but I'd end up hurting myself or the object. Instead, I reach for a little plastic pry tool that is built to do the job right. A competent contractor carriers around a fully loaded tool kit, full of individual tools, not just a tiny foldable all-in-one.

Dear Reader, I encourage you to examine what in your life is a Leatherman, and how that functionality can be broken out into task-specific objects. Think about how you can improve your life by finding single-task objects or units to replace a catch-all. Chances are, the nice set of individual tools you find will not only be higher quality, produce better results, and be more pleasant to use, but also keep you focused on the task at hand, and in the end, make you happier.

item.title: A Comparison That Struck Me
item.description:
A thought struck me (ow) today preparing for guests.

The way our state and federal governments in the US have been handling the COVID-19 pandemic is akin to an abusive partner (or spouse, parent, what have you). Here's how.

  • Telling you to stay home forever
  • Preventing you from going to a job, cutting off your financial independence
